narrative: As a Black male applicant with a strong family income background and plans to attend full-time, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile—including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 37 (slightly above the 36.70 average), and school decile rankings from early years—and predicted an 82% chance that you would fail the bar exam initially. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the average fifth-decile performance—and signaled potential struggles with sustained academic rigor needed for the bar. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, thanks to it being a bit stronger than what the model typically sees. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed toward the failure outlook, as it was lower than the average and highlighted early challenges that carried weight in the model's eyes. Compared to typical students who pass, your lower deciles stand out as weaker relative to their mid-range performances, while your LSAT provided a modest boost many others lack. This combination outweighed positives like your high family income bracket and full-time commitment, which are common among approved applicants (over 92% go full-time). While these results are tough, especially given the extra hurdles Black students like yourself often face in competitive academic pipelines, they reflect patterns from historical data rather than your full potential. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to push it higher or bolstering your academic record through postgraduate courses to improve those decile equivalents. Consider targeted bar prep programs proven effective for underrepresented students, and reapply next cycle with a narrative highlighting your resilience and any upward trends. Many in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas head-on—you can too.
